explanation:
Applied physics solves everyday problems: from free fall to household electricity. Formulas validated through centuries of experimentation.

## How It's Calculated

This calculator applies the C1V1=C2V2 formula with your input values, checking for typical ranges and alerting you when values seem unreasonable.

## Important Notes

This calculation is for reference only. For critical decisions (medical, chemical, or legal), consult with a specialized professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.a: C1 is initial concentration, V1 is initial volume, C2 is final concentration, and V2 is final volume. The formula shows that moles of solute remain constant during dilution.

faq.q: How much water do I add to dilute a solution?
faq.a: Subtract your initial volume from your final volume. For example, to make 100 mL from 10 mL of stock solution, add 90 mL of water.

faq.q: Does this formula work for all types of solutions?
faq.a: It works for any concentration unit (molarity, percentage, parts per million) as long as the unit is proportional. For gases, use PV=nRT instead.

faq.q: How do I prepare a 0.1M solution from a 1M stock?
faq.a: Take 10 mL of stock solution and add 90 mL of water to reach 100 mL total volume. The formula gives V2 = (C1 × V1) / C2 = (1 × 10) / 0.1 = 100 mL.

faq.q: What happens to the number of moles when diluting?
faq.a: Moles stay the same—they just get distributed across a larger volume. This is why C1V1 equals C2V2.

faq.q: Can I use this for serial dilution?
faq.a: Yes, apply the formula for each dilution step in sequence. Each step uses the previous result as the new C1.
Yes, we use standard internationally-validated formulas. For critical applications (pharmaceutical, medical, research), verify results with a qualified professional.

faq.q: Is the calculator free and is my data private?
faq.a: Yes, it's completely free with no registration. All calculations run in your browser—we never store or transmit your data.
